coredata.dc:title: Label-free and highly selective MOFs-based dopamine detection in urine of Parkinson’s patients
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Wenyue
Yin, Yanying
Gu, Ruoheng
Xu, Jingyue
Su, Xing
Wang, Yang
Liu, Ruina
Liu, Xiaozhi
Huang, Jianyong
coredata.dc:description: The amount of dopamine (DA) in the body is closely related to the occurrence and development of Parkinson's disease. However, DA detection in real body fluids is still challenging. Here, we developed a high-selectivity and high-sensitivity label-free lanthanide metal organic frameworks (MOFs) to monitor DA in urine samples from Parkinson’s patients. The fluorescent MOF Eu-α-cyclodextrin (CD), prepared by biomineralization under mild conditions, exhibited DA concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity via host–guest complexation. Furthermore, molecular dynamics (MD) simulation analyses quantitatively dissect thermodynamic interactions of cyclodextrin and guest molecules, which systematically reveals the specific recognition mechanism of cyclodextrin and dopamine molecules. A good response for DA in the range of 10-9 to 10-4 M and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.65 nM were obtained, consistent with the detection range of DA in a variety of biological fluids. Because of the good anti-interference properties of this complex, visual test strips were prepared by the in-situ growth of Eu-αCD nanoparticles on a nitrocellulose (NC) membrane at room temperature, owing to the protein absorbability of NC membranes. The resultant test strips possess the potential for semiquantitative detection of DA by the naked eye. Moreover, we applied Eu-αCD nanoparticles to analyze clinical urine samples from Parkinson's patients, and achieved a low relative error compared with those of commercial HPLC methods. Our work offers an efficient strategy for visual and on-site detection of DA in the clinic, which can assist in early diagnosis of Parkinson's disease.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide MOFs
Test strip
Dopamine
Biomimetic mineralization
Urine samples
Parkinson's disease

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In situ growth of Eu-αCD nanoparticles on NC membranes
Visual detection of DA with Eu-αCD@NC membrane
Analyzing the urine of Parkinson’s patients
Characterization of Eu-αCD nanoparticles and Eu-αCD@NC membranes
DA-induced fluorescence quenching of Eu-αCD nanoparticles
Test strip for visual DA detection
Analyses of DA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation process for (a) Eu-αCD nanoparticles and (b) Eu-αCD@NC membrane.

SEM images of (a) Eu-αCD nanoparticles, (b) an NC membrane and (c) an Eu-αCD@NC membrane. Optical photographs of (d) an NC membrane, (e) a BSA@NC membrane and (f) an Eu-αCD@NC membrane under 365 nm UV light. (g), (h) and (i) Atomic force microscopy images corresponding to the images in(d), (e) and (f), respectively.

(a) The three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of Eu-αCD nanoparticles. (b) Emission fluorescence spectrum of the Eu-αCD nanoparticles; the inset photograph shows Eu-αCD nanoparticle powder under bright-field light and 365 nm UV light. (c) The CIE color coordinate (0.5764, 0.3493). (d) The optical stability test of Eu-αCD nanoparticles with fluorescence intensity (the inset shows emission spectra of Eu-αCD nanoparticles at different times). (e) Fluorescence attenuation curve of the Eu-αCD nanoparticles.

(a) Schematic of DA-induced quenching of Eu-αCD nanoparticles. (b) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-αCD nanoparticles with DA concentrations of 0 ∼ 10-4 M. (c) Relative changes in fluorescence intensities of the Eu-αCD nanoparticles treated with the same concentration of BSA, KCl, NaCl, GL, HSA, Ser, Cys, UA, Urea, AA, Tyr and DA, respectively. (d) The curve of DA concentration versus fluorescence intensity of Eu-αCD nanoparticles. (e) A curve of the F0/Fi ratio versus DA concentration. (f) and (g) ITC curve of the recognition reaction and the corresponding integral curve, respectively. The ITC experiment was performed by titrating the nanoparticles dropwise into a 0.3 mM DA solution.

(a) Corresponding fluorescence spectrogram of the Eu-αCD@NC membrane with different DA concentrations (DA concentrations of 0, 10-7, 10-6, 10-5, and 10-4 M). (b) CIE color coordinates of the Eu-αCD@NC membrane and Eu-αCD nanoparticles with different concentrations of DA (data for the Eu-αCD@NC membrane are represented by crosses, and data for the Eu-αCD nanoparticles are represented by dots). (c) Trend curve of the DA-dependent fluorescence intensity of the Eu-αCD@NC membrane. The insets show optical photographs of the Eu-αCD@NC membrane with different concentrations of DA under 365 nm UV light. (d) Molecular dynamics simulations showing spatio-temporal processes of pulling out the guest molecules (i.e., DA, UA and AA) from cyclodextrin under a constant external force exerted on these guest molecules, respectively. For clarity, the ambient water molecules were not shown in the snapshots that were taken at 0 ps, 30 ps, 60 ps, 170 ps and 250 ps, respectively.

(a) Flowchart of patient urine treatment and the DA detection process. (b) The DA concentration in three groups of real urine samples was measured by this system and a commercial HPLC method. (c) A comparison of the DA detection capabilities of the developed probes and existing fluorescence sensors, where Reference A indicates the developed probe system whereas References B ∼ K denote the reported fluorescence sensors as listed in Table 2. The green dots represent the DA detection limits while the vertical line segments show their respective detection range. The light green, light pink, and light blue areas show the application ranges for urine, plasma, and cerebrospinal fluid, respectively. Table 2 lists the specific references information related to the labels in the x-axis.
Visual test strip based on MOFs to detect DA in urine samples of Parkinson's patients.
The determination of DA in human artificial urine (n = 3).
Comparisons of detection capabilities of the developed probe system and existing DA fluorescence sensors.

head.ce:title: Label-free and highly selective MOFs-based dopamine detection in urine of Parkinson’s patients

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Material Science, Langfang Normal University, Langfang 065000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Material Science, Langfang Normal University, Langfang 065000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The amount of dopamine (DA) in the body is closely related to the occurrence and development of Parkinson's disease. However, DA detection in real body fluids is still challenging. Here, we developed a high-selectivity and high-sensitivity label-free lanthanide metal organic frameworks (MOFs) to monitor DA in urine samples from Parkinson’s patients. The fluorescent MOF Eu-α-cyclodextrin (CD), prepared by biomineralization under mild conditions, exhibited DA concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity via host–guest complexation. Furthermore, molecular dynamics (MD) simulation analyses quantitatively dissect thermodynamic interactions of cyclodextrin and guest molecules, which systematically reveals the specific recognition mechanism of cyclodextrin and dopamine molecules. A good response for DA in the range of 10-9 to 10-4 M and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.65 nM were obtained, consistent with the detection range of DA in a variety of biological fluids. Because of the good anti-interference properties of this complex, visual test strips were prepared by the in-situ growth of Eu-αCD nanoparticles on a nitrocellulose (NC) membrane at room temperature, owing to the protein absorbability of NC membranes. The resultant test strips possess the potential for semiquantitative detection of DA by the naked eye. Moreover, we applied Eu-αCD nanoparticles to analyze clinical urine samples from Parkinson's patients, and achieved a low relative error compared with those of commercial HPLC methods. Our work offers an efficient strategy for visual and on-site detection of DA in the clinic, which can assist in early diagnosis of Parkinson's disease.

ce:section.ce:para: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a neurodegenerative disease with a high incidence in the elderly, and it poses a great threat to the health of the elderly [1]. Dopamine (DA), an important neurotransmitter, is usually maintained at a certain concentration range in the body. An imbalance in the brain stem's substantia nigra leads to a decrease in DA synthesis [2]. A lack of DA has been reported to cause PD, resulting in significantly lower levels of DA in the urine of people with PD than in the urine of healthy people (424 ∼ 2612 nmol/24 hr). The rapid, simple and highly selective determination of DA is crucial for the early diagnosis of PD and the adjustment of drug dosage, which has considerable clinical and pharmacological significance.

Electrochemical determination [3–6], capillary electrophoresis [7], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], fluorescence spectrometry [9] and other precision methods [10] have excellently been developed for DA detection in recent years. These methods are effective, but most of them still require complex procedures. Although the electrochemical determination methods have realized efficient and highly sensitive DA detection, most electrode materials involved require complicated preparation processes and environmentally harmful reagents, as listed in Table S1. The fluorescence-based determination [11–13], with the advantages of fast response, high sensitivity and low cost, is a promising method for DA detection. Some fluorescent quantum dots have been reported for DA detection, and good results have been obtained in such experiments. Nevertheless, most fluorescent quantum dots require a tedious modification process to achieve specific recognition of DA [14], and they are easily quenched by various micromolecules [15,16], severely limiting their application in real body fluids. In comparison with traditional fluorescent quantum dots, metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are considered to be better materials owing to their ideal advantages of stable fluorescence properties and adjustable emission spectra [17–21].

In this work, inspired by biomineralization, we develop high-selectivity and high-sensitivity label-free lanthanide MOFs (Ln-MOFs) by applying bovine serum albumin (BSA) as an inducer for nucleation to monitor DA in urine samples from Parkinson’s patients. Eu-αCD MOF nanoparticles with excitable fluorescence are self-assembled from α-cyclodextrin (CD) and the lanthanide metal Eu3+ through BSA-induced mineralization, which overcomes the limitation of hydrothermal synthesis of Ln-MOFs. The label-free Eu-αCD nanoparticles behave as fluorescent probes to sense DA concentrations as a result of host–guest complexation. Its response range to DA is highly consistent with the urinary DA threshold in Parkinson's patients, implying that it has a high sensitivity for the urinary DA detection. Likewise, the Eu-αCD nanoparticle-based detection has good anti-interference ability when it is utilized to deal with complex body fluids, and its test precision is better than that of commercial high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)-based methods. Compared to probes in aqueous solutions, paper-based sensors and monitoring devices are in great demand for medical diagnosis and point-of-care testing [22–25]. To make clinical tests more portable, we further design visual test strips with good anti-interference via in-situ growth of Eu-αCD nanoparticles on a nitrocellulose (NC) membrane under mild conditions. The Eu-αCD@NC test strips can realize semiquantitative detection by naked-eyes, ensuring long-term consistency of detection results. Thus, label-free Eu-αCD probes offer better accuracy for DA detection in clinical urine samples and allow visual monitor via paper-based sensors for point-of-care testing.

Europium nitrate Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and alpha-cyclodextrin (αCD) with a molar ratio of 2:1 were dissolved in deionized (DI) water, respectively. Then, 0.5 mg·ml−1 BSA was added to the alpha-cyclodextrin solution and stirred until dissolved. Next, the Eu(NO3)3·6H2O solution and the αCD solution containing BSA were mixed, following the addition of NH3·H2O in a drop-by-drop way to adjust the pH of the solution to 8 ∼ 9 (Fig. 1a). The suspended turbidity solution was continuously stirred at room temperature for 1 h, then isolated by centrifugation and washed three times with DI water. The resulting products were dried at 37 °C to obtain the luminescent Eu-αCD nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ growth of Eu-αCD nanoparticles on NC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: 5% BSA solution was prepared in a PBS buffer. As shown in Fig. 1b, the NC membrane was immersed in the BSA solution and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Then, it was dried in an incubator at 37 °C. Next, the mixed solution containing Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and αCD with a molar ratio of 2:1 was prepared to soak the BSA@NC membrane, following by adding NH3·H2O dropwise to adjust the pH of the solution to 8 ∼ 9. After continuous shaking reaction for 1 h and drying in a humid chamber 37 °C, the Eu-αCD@ NC membranes were finally obtained.

In a typical assay, different concentrations of DA were added to Eu-αCD aqueous solution, in which the final concentrations of DA were 0, 10-9, 10-8, 10-7, 10-6, 10-5, and 10-4 M. Fluorescence spectra were recorded after reaction for 5 min. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of DA with Eu-αCD@NC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: For the visual detection of DA, Eu-αCD@NC was added to prepared solutions with different concentrations of DA (2 ml; 0, 10-7, 10-6, 10-5, and 10-4 M). Following a 5 min reaction, the Eu-αCD@NC membranes were removed and dried, and then placed under a UV lamp to observe the color difference. Subsequently, the corresponding fluorescence spectra were acquired.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyzing the urine of Parkinson’s patients
ce:section.ce:para: We collected 24-hr urine samples from Fifth Central Hospital of Tianjin (i.e., Peking University Binhai Hospital). The entire study protocol was approved by the ethics committee of Fifth Central Hospital of Tianjin and written informed consent was obtained from all the patients. The crystals and precipitates in the urine were removed by high-speed centrifugation (12000 rpm, 10 min). Then, 0.1 mM MgCl2 was added and allowed to react for 2 h to avoid interference from porphyrins in the urine. The urine was added to the Eu-αCD solution for 5 min and the spectral intensity was measured; Subsequently, a standard equation was applied to the results to determine the concentration of DA in these real samples (See Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Eu-αCD nanoparticles and Eu-αCD@NC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared Eu-αCD nanoparticles and Eu-αCD@NC membranes were quantitatively characterized with scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and atomic force microscopy (AFM), respectively. The surface analysis was implemented via energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) to quantify chemical compositions of the synthetic Eu-αCD nanoparticles. The results are depicted in Fig. S1. Eu, C, O, and N were derived from europium nitrate and the organic ligand αCD, indicating that Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and αCD were successfully synthesized into crystalline Eu-αCD nanoparticles. It can be observed in Fig. 2 that the Eu-αCD nanoparticles have classic spherical crystal shapes with an average diameter of 200–300 nm. Furthermore, FTIR spectroscopic analysis was applied to determine the functional groups formed in the Eu-αCD nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. S2, a comparison of the FTIR spectra of the ligand αCDs with those of the Eu-αCD nanoparticles confirms the synthesis of the Eu-αCD nanoparticles. More specifically, the peaks at 3400–3500 cm−1 and 1025 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of OH and C-O, respectively. In addition, a new peak at 710 cm−1 is observed in the infrared spectrum, which may come from the new absorption peak brought by BSA. The shift of the peak at 1074 cm−1 may be caused by the coordination between C-OH and Eu3+ [26].

Inspired by biomineralization [27,28], we introduced BSA as an inducer of Eu-αCD nanoparticles. The hydroxyl groups (–OH) of the organic ligand αCDs serve as coordinating groups for Eu-αCD nanoparticles. The O atoms coordinate with Eu ions as H atoms dissociate from –OH. Moreover, owing to the abundant groups of BSA, the coordination ability of BSA must be taken into consideration, as BSA can participate in the construction of Eu-αCD nanoparticles by means of bioconjugation, infiltration or encapsulation. Similar to biomimetic mineralization, BSA-induced fabrication of Eu-αCD nanoparticles occurs with a quick response time under mild conditions. In comparison with pure MOFs, the Eu-αCD nanoparticles (protein − metal − organic frameworks) have a complex structure, where crystalline and amorphous regions coexist [29,30] so that it is usually hard to characterize. As shown in Fig. S3, there are two noteworthy crystallization peaks in the XRD spectrum of the Eu-αCD@NC membrane. These results confirm that the Eu-αCD nanoparticles with BSA were assembled successfully.

Visual effect images of the NC membrane before and after Eu-αCD attachment are shown in Fig. 2. As shown in Fig. 2, we characterized the surface morphology of the NC membrane before and after the in-situ growth of Eu-αCD nanoparticles. As seen in the SEM images, the untreated NC film has obvious fiber holes; However, the holes are covered after 1 hr in-situ growth of Eu-αCD nanoparticles. There are obvious agglomerated crystalline grains on the Eu-αCD@NC membrane, as seen by the many rounded humps in Fig. 2i, with a mean surface roughness (Ra) of 520 nm. The NC membrane and BSA@NC membrane show Ra values of 370 nm and 447 nm and exhibit less difference in color than the Eu-αCD@NC membrane. The desirable surface morphology could provide higher accuracy for the visual detection of DA.

To determine the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths, the three-dimensional fluorescent scanning was carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the density of the contour line shows that when the ordinate is 391 nm, there are two obvious dense points on the abscissa, at 590 and 615 nm. It is worth mentioning that the apparent emission peaks at 590 and 615 nm are caused by the Eu-metal nodes upon 391 nm excitation. Moreover, Fig. 3b shows the fluorescence spectrum of Eu-αCD nanoparticles in the 550 to 675 nm wavelength range, exhibiting the same spectral properties as the three-dimensional scan. The strongest emission in the fluorescent spectra is the 5D0 → 7F2 transition at 615 nm for the Eu3+ ions. Other characteristic transitions are also evident at 590 and 651 nm, which are attributed to the 5D0 → 7F1 and 5D0 → 7F3 transitions, respectively [31,32]. Correspondingly, 1931-CIE color coordinates were measured (0.5764, 0.3493) in an aqueous solution at room temperature, as exhibited in Fig. 3c. These coordinates are used to represent the color in the pure red region of the chromaticity diagram. In brief, Eu-αCD nanoparticles with brilliant red fluorescence resist autofluorescence interference, presenting satisfactory potential for use as biosensors. Besides, it can be found that the Eu-αCD probes have good photostability because its emission spectrum basically does not change with time, as confirmed in Fig. 3d. Quantum yield (QY) was measured by a time-resolved and steady state fluorescence spectrometer FLS 980 (Edinburgh). The absolute photoluminescence QY was quantified via the following expression.
(1)
Q Y =
∫
L
emission
∫
E
solvent
- ∫
E
sample
where
L
emission
was the number of photons emitted by the Eu-αCD probes, whereas
E
sample
and
E
solvent
were the numbers of photons used to excite the probes and the solvent (DI water), respectively. There was the strongest emission intensity (QY = 10.77%) when the excitation wavelength was fixed at 391 nm. Fig. 3e presents the fluorescence attenuation curve measured with the fluorescence spectrometer using the excitation wavelength of 391 nm. By numerically fitting the attenuation curve with the following time-related exponential decay equation, i.e.,
(2)
R
t
= B
e
-
t τ
We found that the fluorescence lifetime of the probes was τ = 211.7676 μs, approximately.

It is well-known that, with a toroidal form containing a hydrophobic inner cavity and a hydrophilic outer side, CD is thought to be an appropriate molecular receptor with high molecular selectivity and enantioselectivity [33]. In fact, there is evidence indicating that the formation of CD-rare earth complexes readily generate an antenna effect with Eu3+ ions via energy transfer [32,34–39], which significantly improves the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ ions. Particularly, the electrons transfer from the ground state (S0) to the excited state (S1) after light excitation, and then transition to excited triple state (T1). Subsequently, the excitation energy is transferred to the Eu3+ excited state through intramolecular energy transfer. Finally, the excited Eu3+ returns to the ground state and thus emits characteristic fluorescence.

The electron transfer induced by the host–guest interactions is the main mechanism leading to material quenching (Fig. 4 a). It is worth noting in this system that the DA-mediated Eu-αCD quenching may be due to dual interactions of the host–guest complexation between DA and CD [40]. DA can effectively reduce the emission intensity of the Eu-αCD nanoparticles, which is probably due to the electron transfer after DA is incorporated into the CD cavity, as illustrated in Fig. 4a. when such inclusions are formed, charge transfer occurs between the lone pair electrons in the oxygen-containing groups of Eu-αCD nanoparticles and the benzoquinone moieties of DA [41]. Therefore, quinones accept electrons and deliver them from the lowest unoccupied molecule orbital (LUMO) to the highest occupied molecule orbital (HOMO).

Moreover, the energy transfer from CD to Eu3+ ions is weakened, resulting in a decrease in the emission at 615 nm. Interestingly, the Eu-αCD nanoparticles show high selectivity for DA detection (Fig. 4c). It is believed that the highly selective recognition of DA originates from synergy between the antenna effect of Eu3+ ions and specific electron donors of the CD nanoparticles. Additionally, it is likely that the inclusion complexes exert an influence on the framework and orientation of the Eu-αCD nanoparticles during the energy transfer of CD- Eu3+ ions and the electron transfer of CD-DA [42], causing excessive ligands to change from ordered to disordered arrangement [43] or inhibiting efficient radiative paths. These factors might also lead to fluorescence quenching of the Eu-αCD nanoparticles. In order to detect binding ability between DA and the Eu-αCD nanoparticles, we further employed isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) to directly measure the heat of the recognition responses, as shown in Fig. 4f. It was observed that there existed a large amount of exotherm with association constant Kd of 2.6 mM after the reactants were added dropwise in experiments, indicating that there was a specific recognition between DA and the Eu-αCD probes, which in turn induced fluorescence quenching of the probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DA-induced fluorescence quenching of Eu-αCD nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the potential of the Eu-αCD nanoparticles for DA monitoring, DA-induced fluorescence measurements were carried out. The Eu-αCD nanoparticles were dispersed in DI water as a standard suspension, and DA was added with a gradually increasing concentration to monitor the emissive response of Eu-αCD nanoparticles. With the addition of DA, the fluorescence intensity of the samples exhibited a significant dose-dependent decrease (Fig. 4). The fluorescence intensity decreased slowly with the DA concentration at 10-9 ∼ 10-7 M; Nevertheless, a sharp downward trend was observed from 10-7 to 10-4 M. This decreasing trend of the fluorescence intensity is significant for the analysis of DA levels in solution. Note that the fluorescence intensity was quenched by ∼ 63.7% from 4165 to 2654 in the 10-7 ∼ 10-6 M DA range in Fig. 4d, which is just within the threshold value in the urine of Parkinson's patients. DA at a concentration of 10-4 M almost completely quenched the fluorescence intensity of Eu-αCD nanoparticles, demonstrating that DA is an efficient quencher in this system.

Based on the detection effect of DA, we determined the correlation between the fluorescence intensity at 615 nm and the DA concentration, which ranged from 10-9 to 10-4 M, as shown in Fig. 4d where exhibited a decreasing exponential relationship (R2 = 0.994). To determine DA concentration in real urine samples more conveniently and accurately by applying the equation for this curve, we transformed the vertical coordinate into F0/Fi ratio, where F0 is the Eu-αCD probe fluorescence intensity without DA. The equation of the final curve is F0/Fi = 2198.789–2197.789/(1+(CDA/0.0422) ^ 0.71929), R2 = 0.94.

As proof of the selectivity of this sensing system, we investigated the changes in the fluorescence intensity of the Eu-αCD nanoparticles in the presence of various molecules and metal ions, which are naturally present in biological samples. The assays were compared with that of DA at the same concentration (5*10-5 M), and the results are presented in Fig. 4c. As expected, BSA, KCl, NaCl, glucose (GL), HSA, serine (Ser), L-cysteine (Cys), uric acid (UA), urea and tyrosine (Tyr) had almost no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the Eu-αCD probes when they were compared with the blank group. However, DA might significantly quench the fluorescence emitted by the probes. We subsequently chose uric acid (UA) and ascorbic acid (AA) contained in urine as similar substances to compare with DA, which showed that UA and AA only caused decreases in relatively fluorescence intensities by 7.73% and 8.83% respectively, while DA with the same concentration could lead to a decrease by 74.83% (Fig. 4c).

Further, we introduced molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to dissect thermodynamic interactions of cyclodextrin and the three guest molecules (i.e., DA, UA and AA) in water. To this end, we constructed the molecular models of cyclodextrin and the corresponding guest molecules including DA, UA and AA, based on the well-developed MD software of LAMMPS (https://www.lammps.org/) in the current simulation analyses, as displayed in Fig. S4. The size of simulation box was set as 2.0 × 2.0 × 3.0 nm. We considered the classical Lennard-Jones potentials
E
LJ
and long-range coulombic interaction potential
E C
in the MD analyses, which took on the following forms, respectively,
(3)
E
LJ
=
4 ε
σ r
12
-
σ r
6
, r <
r c
0 , r >
r c

and.
(4)
E C
=
C
q i
q j
r c
2
, r <
r c
0 , r >
r c
where r was the distance between two atoms; rc was the corresponding cut off distance;
ε
was the depth of potential well; σ was the distance between two bodies at the bottom of the potential energy trap; C = 8.9876 × 109N·m2/C2 was the coulomb constant; qi and qj denoted the amounts of charges of two interacted atoms.

For simplicity, we mimicked the spatio-temporal dynamics of the three guest molecules escaping from a cyclodextrin molecule, respectively. At the initial moment, we placed the guest molecules into the center of the cyclodextrin, respectively. We employed the minimum gradient algorithms to optimize the molecular structures, which carried out the MD simulations based on the canonical ensemble with an ambient temperature of 300 K. The simulation systems could arrive at equilibrium states after running for at least 200 ps, followed by a “pull-out” process to test the specified interactions between cyclodextrin and the corresponding guest molecules. In practice, we exerted a series of external forces, i.e., 1.5, 1.8, 2.2, 2.7 and 3.0 kcal/mol/Å, on the three guest molecules to detect their “pull-out” processes, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5 g and Movie S1. At the same time, we estimated the interaction energy between cyclodextrin and the guest molecules as.
(5)
E =
∫
0
x
F
int
( x )
d x
where
F
int
( x )
was the interaction force between cyclodextrin and a specific gust molecule during the pull-out” process, and × denoted the centroid displacement of guest molecule.

Fig. S5 presents the simulation results about the interaction energies between cyclodextrin and the three guest molecules, i.e., DA, UA and AA. One can find that the interaction energy required for pulling out DA from cyclodextrin was 6 ∼ 7 times larger than that necessary for UA and AA, suggesting that DA is much more difficult to escape from the cyclodextrin molecular structure. Meanwhile, the simulations demonstrated that the time required for a DA molecule to escape from the cyclodextrin structure was much longer than those required for the remaining two kinds of guest molecules (i.e., UA and AA), as illustrated in Fig. 5d and Movie S1. All the facts implied that, in comparison with other guest molecules with similar structures such as UA and AA, the DA molecule could be specifically recognized by the developed Eu-αCD nanoparticles.

Also, we further verified the influence of various metal ions and ambient pH on the Eu-αCD nanoparticles. It turned out that 0.01 mM Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+ and Zn2+ had little effect on the fluorescent intensity of Eu-αCD nanoparticles, as exhibited in Fig. S7, and that the pH varying from 6 to 8.5 which was consistent with human urine samples, only induced a slight fluctuation of 0.11%∼3.5% in the fluorescence intensity emitted by the Eu-αCD nanoparticles, demonstrating that the developed probes were essentially less sensitive to changes in the ambient pH (Fig. S6). Taken together, the synthesized α-CD-coordinated Eu3+ probes possessed high selectivity for DA and was particularly suitable for DA detection based on human urine samples. Furthermore, we systematically evaluate the accuracy of the probes with the aid of human artificial urine, as listed in Table 1 which presented five typical testing results based on the standard addition method with the DA concentration set as 1 × 10-6, 5 × 10-6, 1 × 10-7, 5 × 10-7 and 5 × 10-9 M, respectively. The average recoveries of DA in the artificial urine were in the range of 100.6%∼117% with their relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 3.8%, which made it suitable for detecting DA in real urine samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test strip for visual DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Modern detection technologies have shifted traditional clinical diagnoses to a point-of-care testing basis [44,45]. This is a long-term trend for diagnostics to be more miniaturized and convenient, and test strips have widely been recognized as device carriers because of their potential applications in low-cost settings [46,47]. On the basis of these results, the remarkable behavior of Eu-αCD nanoparticles encouraged us to fabricate a convenient visual discrimination monitor for DA. The NC membrane and BSA@NC membrane both presented a faint blue color, whereas the Eu-αCD@NC membrane possessed a distinct and uniform red color under UV light at 365 nm, which provides good support for the subsequent visual detection of DA. Using Eu-αCD@NC test strips to monitor DA solutions with concentrations of 0, 10-7, 10-6, 10-5, and 10-4 M, we obtained promising test results, as shown in Fig. 5. The corresponding fluorescence intensity spectrum is shown in Fig. 5c. With increasing DA concentration, the color of the Eu-αCD@NC test strips changed from red to pink and finally to light blue, which can be easily distinguished by the naked eye. We marked three points of concentration in the CIF coordinates, which can clearly detect changes in the position in the CIF coordinates, corresponding to our photographs. Notably, in the fluorescence intensity curves of the paper-based sensor, it is obvious that the fluorescence intensity decreases most obviously from 10-7 to 10-5 M over the whole detection range. Both in solution and on the paper-based sensor, our system maintains the optimal detection range and is consistent with the patient threshold range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the accuracy of this method in analyzing real samples, we tested urine samples from patients with PD (see Table S2 for more details of urine samples) and then compared the corresponding results with those obtained by commercial HPLC. With the patients’ consent and authorization, we measured the DA concentration in the patients’ 24-hr urine samples, as displayed in Fig. 6 b. In the experiments, the urine samples from each patient were equally divided into two parts. One of them was send to TianJin ADICON Clinical Laboratories for HPLC analysis whereas the other was tested using the probe system we developed. The DA concentrations detected by the system were 5.69 × 10-7 M, 2.517 × 10-6 M, 2.53 × 10-6 M, 9.667 × 10-8 M, 4.31 × 10-7 M and 4.65 × 10-7 M, respectively. In comparison with the HPLC detection results (6.45 × 10-7 M, 2.232 × 10-6 M, 2.6962 × 10-6 M, 1.24 × 10-7 M, 5.58 × 10-7 M and 4.3 × 10-7 M), our tested data had a relative error ranging from 6.64% to 22%, implying that our probe system had good accuracy and reliability in detecting the DA levels in human urine.

Fig. 6c and Table 2 provide a further comparison of the DA detection capabilities of the developed probes and existing fluorescence sensors [42,48–56]. One can readily observe that the developed probe system has higher sensitivity and wider versatility for the DA monitoring in various biological environments. In addition, it should be pointed out that the Eu-αCD@NC test strips can recognize the DA molecules in a fast and high-selectivity way thus greatly enhancing the efficiency of DA detection.

A high-selectivity and high-sensitivity label-free MOFs-based fluorescent probe has been developed to specifically recognize DA in human urine samples. The probe can achieve a wide DA detection range from 10-9 to 10-4 M, which makes it essentially feasible to quantify the DA concentration (∼10-7 to 10-5 M) in urine samples from Parkinson’s patients. Also, its accuracy of DA detection in real urine samples is roughly comparable to that given by the classical HPLC. Further, visualized strips made from the fluorescent probes enable us to realize semiquantitative detection visible to the naked eyes, since the color of the test strips vary from bright red to pink to light blue when the DA concentration in urine samples are increased from 0 to 10-4 M. In view of its high selectivity, sensitivity, efficiency and good anti-interference in the DA detection, the developed probe system is especially suitable for the routine detection of DA in urine and potential assays associated with cerebrospinal fluid, plasma and cell imaging, which can thus provide a valuable reference for early diagnosis of clinical diseases such as PD.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (grant numbers 11972001, 11972002, 12072001 and 91848201), Beijing Natural Science Foundation (grant number Z200017), National Key Research and Development Program of China (grant number 2021YFA1000200), Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department (grant number ZD2021092) and Hebei “333 Talent Project” (grant number A202101023).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.136371.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2
